topic.what: Comprehensive ethnopharmacological and pharmacological review of Nigella sativa (black seed; Habbatus Sauda in Islamic/Unani tradition) for gastric ailments, synthesising evidence from Prophetic medicine (Tibb al-Nabawi), classical Unani texts, and modern pharmacology with thymoquinone (TQ) as the primary active constituent.
topic.how: Systematic literature survey covering traditional textual sources (Ibn Sina's Canon, Prophetic hadith), ethnobotanical surveys across Islamic/MENA traditions, and experimental pharmacology studies; integrates phytochemical, in-vitro, in-vivo, and limited clinical evidence.
topic.why_matters: Nigella sativa is one of the most cross-culturally documented medicinal plants in the world — endorsed in Prophetic medicine, codified in Unani materia medica, and now extensively studied pharmacologically; understanding its gastric evidence base illuminates how a single herb traverses religious, classical, and biomedical legitimacy frameworks, making it a model cross-paradigm case for integrative gastroenterology.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Coverage of experimental evidence types supporting gastric applications
characteristics.value: Strong preclinical evidence (multiple animal models, multiple ulcer induction methods); limited but positive clinical data
characteristics.evidence: Review synthesises >50 primary studies across model types
characteristics.context: Systematic survey; clinical trial data sparse but promising

characteristics.what_it_measures: Attribution of gastroprotective activity to TQ fraction
characteristics.value: TQ consistently identified as primary bioactive; fixed oil and other constituents contribute secondarily
characteristics.evidence: Multiple mechanistic studies reviewed; isolation studies confirm TQ activity
characteristics.context: TQ concentration varies significantly by seed origin and extraction method

characteristics.what_it_measures: Depth of endorsement across traditional medical systems
characteristics.value: Documented in Prophetic medicine (hadith), Ibn Sina's Canon, Greek-influenced Unani, and South/Southeast Asian folk traditions
characteristics.evidence: Primary textual citations provided in review
characteristics.context: Religious endorsement adds legitimacy layer absent in purely pharmacological herbs

characteristics.what_it_measures: In-vitro inhibition of H. pylori growth by Nigella sativa extracts
characteristics.value: Moderate inhibitory activity in in-vitro studies; clinical eradication data limited
characteristics.evidence: In-vitro MIC studies reviewed; one small clinical pilot showing adjunctive benefit
characteristics.context: In-vitro activity does not reliably predict in-vivo eradication

good_for: Understanding Nigella sativa as a model cross-paradigm herb bridging Islamic/Prophetic, Unani, and biomedical gastroenterology
Identifying TQ as a mechanistically tractable lead compound for gastroprotection drug discovery
Informing culturally sensitive gastroenterology in Muslim-majority populations where Habbatus Sauda use is prevalent
Generating hypotheses for H. pylori adjunctive therapy with Nigella sativa
not_for: First-line H. pylori eradication — insufficient clinical evidence
Definitive clinical dosing — standardisation of TQ content in commercial products variable
Replacing standard PPI or antibiotic therapy in confirmed H. pylori gastritis
requires: Standardised TQ-quantified Nigella sativa preparations for clinical trials
Large RCTs for gastritis and functional dyspepsia endpoints
Pharmacovigilance data for long-term TQ exposure

novelty: Provides the most comprehensive gastric-focused synthesis of Nigella sativa evidence across Prophetic/Islamic, Unani, and biomedical literature as of 2016
Frames Nigella sativa as a cross-paradigm herb with simultaneous religious, classical, and pharmacological legitimacy — a unique positioning in the medical herb landscape
Aggregates anti-H. pylori preclinical evidence and contextualises it within the broader gastric therapeutic scope, including cytoprotection and anti-inflammatory actions
open_problems: Optimal dose and standardised TQ content for gastric indications not established
RCT evidence for gastritis, dyspepsia, or H. pylori adjuvant therapy lacking
Variability in TQ content across geographic seed sources and preparation methods unresolved
Long-term hepatic and renal safety of therapeutic TQ doses not characterised
Interaction with antibiotics in H. pylori triple therapy not studied clinically
